text_summary: Vemurafenib is an oral selective BRAF V600E kinase inhibitor that received FDA approval in 2011 for unresectable or metastatic melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation. In the BRIM-3 trial, vemurafenib improved OS compared to dacarbazine (13.6 vs 9.7 months) with an ORR of 48%. Vemurafenib binds the ATP-binding site of BRAF V600E with high selectivity. Common adverse effects include arthralgia, rash, photosensitivity, and cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (due to paradoxical MAPK activation). Single-agent vemurafenib has largely been superseded by combination BRAF+MEK inhibition (dabrafenib+trametinib, encorafenib+binimetinib) which provides superior efficacy and reduced paradoxical ERK activation. Resistance develops through NRAS mutations, BRAF amplification, and MEK mutations within 6-12 months on monotherapy.
approved_indications: BRAF V600E-mutant unresectable/metastatic melanoma
mechanism_of_action: Selective ATP-competitive BRAF V600E kinase inhibitor

text_summary: Dabrafenib plus trametinib is a combination BRAF inhibitor plus MEK inhibitor regimen that is a standard of care for BRAF V600E/K-mutant melanoma and NSCLC. FDA approval in melanoma was based on the COMBI-d and COMBI-v trials demonstrating 5-year OS of 34% (unmatched for metastatic melanoma at the time). In BRAF V600E-mutant NSCLC, the combination showed ORR of 64% and median PFS of 10.9 months. The combination reduces paradoxical ERK activation and cutaneous toxicities compared to BRAF inhibitor monotherapy. Key adverse effects include pyrexia (dabrafenib-specific), rash, fatigue, and cardiac effects. Also approved in the adjuvant setting for stage III BRAF V600E/K melanoma (COMBI-AD trial). Trametinib inhibits MEK1/2, blocking downstream signaling even when BRAF feedback reactivation occurs.
approved_indications: BRAF V600E/K melanoma (metastatic and adjuvant), BRAF V600E NSCLC, BRAF V600E anaplastic thyroid cancer
mechanism_of_action: Combined BRAF + MEK1/2 kinase inhibition (vertical pathway blockade)

text_summary: Osimertinib is a third-generation, irreversible, mutant-selective EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor that is the NCCN-preferred first-line therapy for EGFR-mutant advanced NSCLC. In the FLAURA trial, osimertinib demonstrated superior PFS (18.9 vs 10.2 months) and OS (38.6 vs 31.8 months) compared to erlotinib/gefitinib. Osimertinib has excellent CNS penetration, with intracranial ORR of 91% in patients with brain metastases. It targets both common sensitizing EGFR mutations (L858R, exon 19 del) and the T790M resistance mutation. Key adverse effects include rash, diarrhea, and QTc prolongation. Also approved in the adjuvant setting for resected EGFR-mutant NSCLC (ADAURA trial: DFS HR 0.17). Resistance mechanisms include EGFR C797S, MET amplification, HER2 amplification, and small cell transformation. FLAURA2 added chemotherapy to osimertinib for further PFS improvement.
approved_indications: EGFR-mutant NSCLC (first-line, T790M-positive second-line, adjuvant)
mechanism_of_action: Third-generation irreversible mutant-selective EGFR TKI (spares wild-type EGFR)

text_summary: Crizotinib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting ALK, ROS1, and MET that was the first FDA-approved ALK inhibitor for ALK-positive NSCLC (2011) and ROS1-positive NSCLC (2016). In the PROFILE 1014 trial for ALK-positive NSCLC, crizotinib showed PFS of 10.9 months versus 7.0 months for chemotherapy. For ROS1-positive NSCLC, the PROFILE 1001 study demonstrated ORR of 72% and median PFS of 19.3 months. While crizotinib was a breakthrough therapy, it has been largely replaced as first-line ALK therapy by alectinib and lorlatinib due to their superior CNS penetration and activity against resistance mutations. Common adverse effects include visual disturbances, nausea, diarrhea, and hepatotoxicity. Crizotinib has limited blood-brain barrier penetration compared to next-generation ALK inhibitors.
approved_indications: ALK-positive NSCLC, ROS1-positive NSCLC
mechanism_of_action: ATP-competitive multi-kinase inhibitor (ALK/ROS1/MET)

text_summary: Alectinib is a highly selective, CNS-penetrant ALK inhibitor and the NCCN-preferred first-line therapy for ALK-positive advanced NSCLC. The ALEX trial demonstrated dramatically superior PFS for alectinib versus crizotinib (34.8 months vs 10.9 months) with intracranial ORR of 81%. The J-ALEX trial in Japanese patients confirmed the benefit with PFS not reached at 12 months for alectinib. Alectinib has excellent blood-brain barrier penetration, making it the preferred choice for patients with baseline brain metastases. The drug is well tolerated with main adverse effects being myalgia, peripheral edema, and elevated liver enzymes. Alectinib is also active against some ALK resistance mutations (L1196M, I1171T) but not the G1202R solvent-front mutation. The ALINA trial established alectinib in the adjuvant setting for resected ALK-positive NSCLC.
approved_indications: ALK-positive NSCLC (first-line, adjuvant)
mechanism_of_action: Highly selective ATP-competitive ALK inhibitor with CNS penetration

text_summary: Lorlatinib is a third-generation, macrocyclic ALK/ROS1 inhibitor with broad activity against most known ALK resistance mutations, including the challenging G1202R solvent-front mutation. The CROWN trial established lorlatinib as a first-line option for ALK-positive NSCLC, with 3-year PFS rate of 64% versus 19% for crizotinib. Lorlatinib has the highest CNS penetration among ALK inhibitors, with 82% intracranial ORR. It is particularly valuable after progression on second-generation ALK TKIs (alectinib, ceritinib, brigatinib). Key adverse effects include hypercholesterolemia, hypertriglyceridemia, CNS effects (cognitive changes, mood changes), and weight gain. Lorlatinib also has activity against compound ALK mutations that arise under sequential TKI pressure. The drug is an important salvage option in the ALK TKI sequence and is being investigated in first-line combinations.
approved_indications: ALK-positive NSCLC (first-line and after prior ALK TKI)
mechanism_of_action: Third-generation macrocyclic ALK/ROS1 inhibitor with broad resistance-mutation coverage

text_summary: Sotorasib (AMG 510) is the first FDA-approved KRAS G12C covalent inhibitor, marking a milestone in targeting a historically undruggable oncogene. Approval for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC was based on the CodeBreaK 100 trial demonstrating ORR of 37.1% and median PFS of 6.8 months. Sotorasib irreversibly locks KRAS G12C in its inactive GDP-bound state, preventing nucleotide exchange and downstream RAS signaling. The CodeBreaK 200 randomized trial confirmed superiority over docetaxel in previously treated NSCLC. Key adverse effects include diarrhea, nausea, hepatotoxicity (elevated ALT/AST), and musculoskeletal pain. Resistance mechanisms include secondary KRAS mutations (Y96D, R68S, H95), MET amplification, and MAPK pathway reactivation. Combination strategies with anti-EGFR antibodies in CRC (CodeBreaK 101) and with immunotherapy are under investigation.
approved_indications: KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC (after prior systemic therapy)
mechanism_of_action: Irreversible covalent KRAS G12C inhibitor (locks in GDP-bound inactive state)

text_summary: Adagrasib (MRTX849) is a covalent KRAS G12C inhibitor with a long half-life of 23 hours, enabling sustained target engagement. FDA approval for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC was based on the KRYSTAL-1 trial demonstrating ORR of 42.9% and median PFS of 6.5 months. Adagrasib demonstrates CNS penetration, with intracranial ORR of 33% in patients with brain metastases, a potential advantage over sotorasib. Key adverse effects include nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, fatigue, and hepatotoxicity. In the KRYSTAL-1 CRC cohort, adagrasib combined with cetuximab showed ORR of 46%, significantly higher than monotherapy (19%). The KRYSTAL-7 trial is evaluating adagrasib in combination with pembrolizumab in first-line KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC. Like sotorasib, resistance involves secondary KRAS mutations and bypass pathway activation.
approved_indications: KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC (after prior systemic therapy)
mechanism_of_action: Irreversible covalent KRAS G12C inhibitor with long half-life and CNS penetration

text_summary: Trastuzumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting the HER2 extracellular domain (domain IV) that revolutionized HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Approval was based on pivotal trials demonstrating significant improvement in DFS and OS when added to chemotherapy in both metastatic and adjuvant settings. Trastuzumab works through multiple mechanisms: inhibition of HER2 signaling, antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), prevention of HER2 extracellular domain shedding, and inhibition of angiogenesis. In the adjuvant setting, trastuzumab for one year reduces breast cancer recurrence by approximately 50%. Trastuzumab is also approved for HER2-positive metastatic gastric cancer in combination with chemotherapy (ToGA trial). Cardiac toxicity (asymptomatic LVEF decline) is the main safety concern, requiring cardiac monitoring. Biosimilar trastuzumab products are now widely available.
approved_indications: HER2-positive breast cancer (adjuvant and metastatic), HER2-positive gastric cancer
mechanism_of_action: Anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody (ADCC, signaling inhibition, shedding prevention)

text_summary: Pembrolizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1 that has become the most widely used immune checkpoint inhibitor across oncology, with over 30 FDA-approved indications spanning 16+ cancer types. Landmark tissue-agnostic approvals include MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors (first biomarker-based approval) and TMB-H solid tumors. In NSCLC, pembrolizumab monotherapy is first-line for PD-L1 TPS >=50% (KEYNOTE-024: PFS 10.3 months) and in combination with chemotherapy regardless of PD-L1 (KEYNOTE-189: OS 22 months). KEYNOTE-522 established pembrolizumab in early TNBC (neoadjuvant + adjuvant). The drug restores anti-tumor immunity by blocking PD-1/PD-L1 interaction. Immune-related adverse events include pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, and nephritis. Pembrolizumab is administered IV every 3 or 6 weeks.
approved_indications: NSCLC, melanoma, HNSCC, urothelial, MSI-H pan-cancer, TMB-H pan-cancer, gastric, cervical, hepatocellular, breast (TNBC), endometrial, renal, cutaneous SCC, Merkel cell
mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody (restores T cell anti-tumor activity)

text_summary: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1, approved across multiple cancer types as monotherapy and in combination with ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4). In advanced melanoma, nivolumab plus ipilimumab achieved a 5-year OS rate of 52% (CheckMate-067 trial). In NSCLC, nivolumab monotherapy is approved as second-line therapy, and the combination with ipilimumab plus limited chemotherapy (CheckMate-9LA) is a first-line option. The CheckMate-227 trial demonstrated nivolumab plus ipilimumab benefit in TMB-high NSCLC. In renal cell carcinoma, nivolumab plus ipilimumab is first-line for intermediate/poor-risk patients (CheckMate-214: 4-year OS 53%). Also approved for hepatocellular, esophageal, gastric, and MSI-H colorectal cancers. The dual checkpoint blockade with ipilimumab provides complementary immune activation but increases toxicity risk.
approved_indications: melanoma, NSCLC, renal, HNSCC, urothelial, hepatocellular, esophageal, gastric, MSI-H CRC, mesothelioma
mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody (restores T cell anti-tumor activity)

text_summary: Atezolizumab is a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-L1, with a modified Fc region to eliminate ADCC. FDA-approved indications include NSCLC, SCLC, urothelial carcinoma, TNBC, hepatocellular carcinoma, and melanoma. In NSCLC, the IMpower150 trial established atezolizumab plus bevacizumab plus chemotherapy as a first-line option with OS of 19.2 months, including patients with EGFR/ALK alterations (who are typically excluded from PD-1 monotherapy trials). In ES-SCLC, atezolizumab plus chemotherapy (IMpower133) improved OS to 12.3 months, becoming the first immunotherapy approved in SCLC. Unlike pembrolizumab and nivolumab which target PD-1, atezolizumab targets PD-L1 specifically, preserving PD-1/PD-L2 interaction. SP142 IHC assay is the companion diagnostic using a different scoring algorithm than PD-L1 TPS.
approved_indications: NSCLC, SCLC, urothelial, TNBC, hepatocellular, melanoma
mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-L1 monoclonal antibody (blocks PD-L1 on tumor and immune cells)

text_summary: Larotrectinib is a highly selective oral TRK inhibitor targeting TRKA, TRKB, and TRKC kinases encoded by NTRK1, NTRK2, and NTRK3 genes respectively. Larotrectinib received the first tissue-agnostic FDA approval for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors based on integrated analysis of the NAVIGATE, SCOUT, and LOXO-TRK-14001 trials, demonstrating ORR of 75% across 17 different cancer types in adults and children. The median duration of response was not reached at 12 months follow-up. Larotrectinib is well tolerated with main adverse effects being fatigue, dizziness, nausea, and transaminase elevation. Resistance mechanisms include NTRK solvent-front mutations (G595R in NTRK1, G623R in NTRK3) and xDFG mutations. Next-generation TRK inhibitors (selitrectinib) are in development to overcome resistance. NTRK fusion testing by NGS (preferably RNA-based) is required for patient selection.
approved_indications: NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (tissue-agnostic)
mechanism_of_action: Selective ATP-competitive TRK (tropomyosin receptor kinase) inhibitor

text_summary: Entrectinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting TRK (NTRK1/2/3), ROS1, and ALK with notable CNS penetration. FDA approval for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors was based on integrated analysis of STARTRK-2, STARTRK-1, and ALKA-372-001 trials showing ORR of 57% across multiple tumor types, with intracranial ORR of 50% in patients with CNS metastases. For ROS1-positive NSCLC, entrectinib showed ORR of 67% with intracranial activity. The CNS penetration advantage makes entrectinib particularly valuable for patients with brain metastases. Common adverse effects include dysgeusia, fatigue, weight gain, diarrhea, and peripheral edema. Entrectinib is more broadly kinase-active than larotrectinib, which may contribute to both its CNS efficacy and its broader adverse event profile. NTRK fusion or ROS1 rearrangement testing is required for patient selection.
approved_indications: NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (tissue-agnostic), ROS1-positive NSCLC
mechanism_of_action: Multi-kinase inhibitor (TRK/ROS1/ALK) with CNS penetration

text_summary: Selpercatinib is a highly selective, CNS-penetrant RET kinase inhibitor approved for RET fusion-positive NSCLC, RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer, and RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer. In the LIBRETTO-001 trial for NSCLC, selpercatinib demonstrated ORR of 64% in previously treated and 84% in treatment-naive patients. For RET-mutant MTC, ORR was 73% in previously treated patients. Selpercatinib was designed to avoid off-target VEGFR2 inhibition, reducing hypertension risk compared to earlier multikinase inhibitors. Key adverse effects include dry mouth, hypertension, fatigue, diarrhea, and peripheral edema. Resistance mechanisms include RET solvent-front mutations (G810R/S/C) and bypass signaling through MET amplification. Selpercatinib is now being investigated as first-line therapy compared to standard chemotherapy plus pembrolizumab in RET fusion-positive NSCLC (LIBRETTO-431).
approved_indications: RET fusion-positive NSCLC, RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer, RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer
mechanism_of_action: Highly selective ATP-competitive RET kinase inhibitor with CNS activity

text_summary: Capmatinib is a highly selective, oral MET inhibitor approved for NSCLC with MET exon 14 skipping mutations. The GEOMETRY mono-1 trial demonstrated ORR of 68% in treatment-naive and 41% in previously treated patients with MET exon 14 skipping. Capmatinib has also shown activity against MET amplification (gene copy number >=10) with ORR of 29%. The drug achieves high systemic and intracranial drug concentrations. Key adverse effects include peripheral edema, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, and increased creatinine. Capmatinib selectively inhibits the MET receptor tyrosine kinase, blocking HGF-dependent and HGF-independent MET activation. Resistance can develop through secondary MET kinase domain mutations (D1228V/N, Y1230H) and bypass signaling. MET exon 14 skipping is detected by RNA-based NGS or DNA-based sequencing of splice site regions.
approved_indications: MET exon 14 skipping mutation-positive NSCLC
mechanism_of_action: Selective ATP-competitive MET receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor

text_summary: Pemigatinib is a selective, oral inhibitor of FGFR1, FGFR2, and FGFR3 approved for previously treated, unresectable cholangiocarcinoma with FGFR2 fusion or other rearrangement. The FIGHT-202 trial demonstrated ORR of 36% with median DOR of 9.1 months in FGFR2 fusion-positive cholangiocarcinoma. Pemigatinib represents the first approved targeted therapy specifically for cholangiocarcinoma. Key adverse effects include hyperphosphatemia (due to on-target FGF23/FGFR1 inhibition), alopecia, diarrhea, fatigue, and dysgeusia. Serous retinal detachment has also been reported. Resistance mechanisms include FGFR2 gatekeeper mutations (V564F) and molecular brake mutations. FGFR2 fusion detection requires NGS (RNA-based testing preferred) as FISH may miss some rearrangement patterns. Pemigatinib is being studied in FGFR-altered urothelial carcinoma and myeloproliferative neoplasms.
approved_indications: FGFR2 fusion-positive cholangiocarcinoma (previously treated)
mechanism_of_action: Selective ATP-competitive FGFR1/2/3 kinase inhibitor

text_summary: Alpelisib is an oral alpha-specific PI3K inhibitor approved in combination with fulvestrant for PIK3CA-mutant, HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer after endocrine therapy progression. The SOLAR-1 trial demonstrated PFS improvement from 5.7 to 11.0 months (HR 0.65) with alpelisib plus fulvestrant versus fulvestrant alone. The therascreen PIK3CA RGQ PCR Kit is the FDA-approved companion diagnostic. Alpelisib selectively inhibits p110-alpha, the catalytic subunit of PI3K encoded by PIK3CA, with minimal inhibition of p110-beta, p110-gamma, and p110-delta isoforms. The key adverse effect is hyperglycemia (up to 60% any grade), requiring pre-treatment HbA1c screening and glucose management. Other adverse effects include rash, diarrhea, nausea, and decreased appetite. PIK3CA hotspot mutations (H1047R, E545K, E542K) are the primary targets for patient selection.
approved_indications: PIK3CA-mutant HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer (with fulvestrant)
mechanism_of_action: Alpha-specific PI3K inhibitor (selective for p110-alpha catalytic subunit)

text_summary: Ivosidenib is a first-in-class oral inhibitor of mutant IDH1, approved for IDH1-mutant relapsed/refractory AML and IDH1-mutant locally advanced or metastatic cholangiocarcinoma. In AML, the AG120-C-001 trial showed complete remission rate of 24.7% in R/R AML patients. In combination with azacitidine for newly diagnosed AML patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, ivosidenib significantly improved overall survival. The ClarIDHy trial established ivosidenib in IDH1-mutant cholangiocarcinoma with PFS of 2.7 vs 1.4 months for placebo (HR 0.37). Ivosidenib inhibits the IDH1 R132H mutant enzyme, reducing production of the oncometabolite D-2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG) and reversing epigenetic changes that block differentiation. Differentiation syndrome (similar to ATRA syndrome) is a known serious adverse effect requiring vigilant monitoring. QTc prolongation is another concern.
approved_indications: IDH1-mutant relapsed/refractory AML, IDH1-mutant cholangiocarcinoma
mechanism_of_action: Mutant IDH1 inhibitor (reduces D-2-hydroxyglutarate oncometabolite production)

text_summary: Enasidenib is an oral selective inhibitor of mutant IDH2, approved for IDH2-mutant relapsed/refractory AML. The AG221-C-001 trial demonstrated an overall response rate of 40.3%, with complete remission rate of 19.3% and median OS of 9.3 months. Enasidenib inhibits the IDH2 mutant enzyme (R140Q, R172K), reducing D-2-hydroxyglutarate production and promoting leukemic blast differentiation. Time to response can be prolonged (median 1.9 months for first response), reflecting the differentiation-based mechanism rather than direct cytotoxicity. Differentiation syndrome occurs in approximately 14% of patients and requires prompt glucocorticoid treatment. Other adverse effects include nausea, hyperbilirubinemia (due to off-target UGT1A1 inhibition), and decreased appetite. IDH2 mutation testing by NGS or targeted PCR is required for patient selection. Enasidenib represents a paradigm of differentiation therapy in AML.
approved_indications: IDH2-mutant relapsed/refractory AML
mechanism_of_action: Mutant IDH2 inhibitor (promotes differentiation by reducing 2-HG)

text_summary: Olaparib is an oral PARP1/2 inhibitor and the first-in-class PARP inhibitor approved across multiple cancer types. Key approvals include: BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer maintenance (SOLO-1: PFS 56 vs 13.8 months), BRCA-mutant metastatic breast cancer (OlympiAD trial), BRCA-mutant metastatic prostate cancer (PROfound trial), and BRCA-mutant pancreatic cancer maintenance (POLO trial). Olaparib exploits synthetic lethality in tumors with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), where PARP inhibition prevents repair of single-strand breaks, leading to lethal double-strand breaks during replication. HRD-positive tumors without BRCA mutations also show benefit. Key adverse effects include anemia, fatigue, nausea, and a small risk of myelodysplastic syndrome/acute myeloid leukemia (MDS/AML). Resistance develops through BRCA reversion mutations, loss of 53BP1/REV7, and restoration of HR function.
approved_indications: BRCA-mutant ovarian, breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancers; HRD-positive ovarian cancer
mechanism_of_action: PARP1/2 inhibitor (synthetic lethality with homologous recombination deficiency)

text_summary: Rucaparib is an oral PARP1/2/3 inhibitor approved for BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer as maintenance therapy and treatment. The ARIEL3 trial demonstrated maintenance PFS of 10.8 months for rucaparib versus 5.4 months for placebo in platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer, with the greatest benefit in BRCA-mutant (median PFS 16.6 months) and HRD-positive subgroups. Rucaparib was the first PARP inhibitor with an FDA-approved companion diagnostic for detecting BRCA mutations in tumor tissue (FoundationFocus CDx BRCA). The drug has broader PARP family inhibition (PARP1/2/3) compared to olaparib. Key adverse effects include anemia, fatigue, nausea, elevated ALT/AST, and thrombocytopenia. Similar to other PARP inhibitors, resistance develops through BRCA reversion mutations restoring homologous recombination. Rucaparib is being investigated in BRCA-mutant prostate and pancreatic cancers.
approved_indications: BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer (treatment and maintenance)
mechanism_of_action: PARP1/2/3 inhibitor (synthetic lethality with HRD)

text_summary: Avapritinib is a selective type I kinase inhibitor specifically designed to target the KIT D816V and PDGFRA D842V activation loop mutations that are resistant to conventional type II kinase inhibitors (imatinib, sunitinib). For PDGFRA D842V-mutant GIST, the NAVIGATOR trial demonstrated ORR of 88% with median DOR not reached, establishing avapritinib as the standard of care for this previously untreatable subtype. For advanced systemic mastocytosis, avapritinib (PATHFINDER trial) showed ORR of 93% in advSM patients. Unlike imatinib and sunitinib that bind the inactive DFG-out conformation (type II), avapritinib binds the active DFG-in conformation (type I), explaining its unique activity against activation loop mutations. Key adverse effects include cognitive effects (memory impairment, cognitive disturbance), periorbital edema, nausea, and intracranial hemorrhage risk.
approved_indications: PDGFRA D842V-mutant GIST, advanced systemic mastocytosis
mechanism_of_action: Type I kinase inhibitor targeting KIT/PDGFRA activation loop mutations (DFG-in binding)

text_summary: Sacituzumab govitecan is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) comprising an anti-Trop-2 antibody linked to SN-38 (active metabolite of irinotecan) via a cleavable CL2A linker with a high drug-to-antibody ratio (approximately 7.6:1). Approved for metastatic TNBC (ASCENT trial: median PFS 5.6 vs 1.7 months, OS 12.1 vs 6.7 months) and HR-positive/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer (TROPiCS-02 trial), sacituzumab govitecan represents a major advance for patients with limited targeted therapy options. Also approved for urothelial carcinoma. Trop-2 is broadly expressed across epithelial cancers, making this platform potentially applicable to many tumor types. The bystander effect from SN-38 release provides activity even in heterogeneously Trop-2-expressing tumors. Key adverse effects include neutropenia, diarrhea, nausea, and alopecia. UGT1A1*28 homozygosity increases toxicity risk.
approved_indications: metastatic TNBC, HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer, urothelial carcinoma
mechanism_of_action: Anti-Trop-2 antibody-drug conjugate with SN-38 payload (topoisomerase I inhibitor)

text_summary: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd, DS-8201a) is a next-generation anti-HER2 antibody-drug conjugate with a cleavable tetrapeptide-based linker, a novel topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DXd), and a high drug-to-antibody ratio of approximately 8:1. In the DESTINY-Breast03 trial, T-DXd showed unprecedented PFS of 25.1 months versus 7.2 months for T-DM1 in HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer. The DESTINY-Breast04 trial expanded the paradigm to HER2-low breast cancer (IHC 1+ or IHC 2+/FISH-negative), demonstrating PFS of 10.1 vs 5.4 months, redefining actionable HER2 levels. Also approved for HER2-positive gastric cancer (DESTINY-Gastric01) and HER2-mutant NSCLC (DESTINY-Lung02). The bystander killing effect from membrane-permeable DXd enables activity in heterogeneous tumors. Interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis is the most concerning adverse effect, requiring careful monitoring.
approved_indications: HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer, HER2-low breast cancer, HER2-positive gastric cancer, HER2-mutant NSCLC
mechanism_of_action: Anti-HER2 antibody-drug conjugate with topoisomerase I inhibitor (DXd) payload and bystander effect

text_summary: Sacituzumab govitecan is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting TROP2, a cell-surface glycoprotein broadly expressed in epithelial cancers. The ADC consists of an anti-TROP2 humanized antibody conjugated to SN-38 (the active metabolite of irinotecan) via a proprietary hydrolyzable CL2A linker with a high drug-to-antibody ratio of approximately 7.6:1. In the ASCENT trial for metastatic TNBC, sacituzumab govitecan demonstrated PFS of 5.6 vs 1.7 months and OS of 12.1 vs 6.7 months compared to physician's choice chemotherapy. The TROPiCS-02 trial extended approval to HR-positive/HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer. Also approved for previously treated locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma. The high DAR and cleavable linker enable potent bystander killing in heterogeneous tumors. Key adverse effects include neutropenia (grade 3-4 in ~50%), diarrhea, nausea, and alopecia. UGT1A1*28 homozygosity increases SN-38 exposure and toxicity risk. No companion diagnostic for TROP2 expression is required.
approved_indications: metastatic TNBC, HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer, locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma
mechanism_of_action: Anti-TROP2 antibody-drug conjugate with SN-38 topoisomerase I inhibitor payload

text_summary: Enfortumab vedotin is an antibody-drug conjugate targeting Nectin-4, a cell adhesion molecule expressed in the majority of urothelial carcinomas. The ADC links an anti-Nectin-4 monoclonal antibody to the microtubule-disrupting agent monomethyl auristatin E (MMAE) via a protease-cleavable valine-citrulline linker. The EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 trial established enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab as the new first-line standard for locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma, with median PFS of 12.5 months and OS not reached, approximately doubling the PFS of gemcitabine/platinum chemotherapy. Enfortumab vedotin monotherapy was previously approved for previously treated urothelial carcinoma based on EV-301 (OS 12.9 vs 9.0 months). Key adverse effects include skin reactions (maculopapular rash, pruritus), peripheral neuropathy, hyperglycemia, and ocular toxicity. No Nectin-4 expression testing is required as the target is ubiquitously expressed in urothelial carcinoma.
approved_indications: locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma (first-line with pembrolizumab, and previously treated monotherapy)
mechanism_of_action: Anti-Nectin-4 antibody-drug conjugate with MMAE microtubule inhibitor payload

text_summary: Mirvetuximab soravtansine is an antibody-drug conjugate targeting folate receptor alpha (FRalpha), a membrane protein highly expressed in ovarian cancer. The ADC consists of an anti-FRalpha antibody conjugated to the maytansinoid DM4 via a cleavable disulfide linker. Mirvetuximab soravtansine received FDA accelerated approval for FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer based on the SORAYA trial (ORR 32.4%, median DOR 6.9 months). The confirmatory MIRASOL trial demonstrated superiority over investigator's choice chemotherapy in FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (PFS 5.6 vs 4.0 months, OS 16.5 vs 12.8 months, HR 0.67). The VENTANA FOLR1 (26B3) RxDx assay is the companion diagnostic, requiring PS2+ score (moderate-to-strong staining in >=75% of cells). Key adverse effects include blurred vision, keratopathy, nausea, fatigue, and peripheral neuropathy. Ocular monitoring with regular eye exams is required.
approved_indications: FRalpha-positive, platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian cancer
mechanism_of_action: Anti-FRalpha antibody-drug conjugate with DM4 maytansinoid payload

text_summary: Zolbetuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2), a tight junction protein aberrantly exposed on the surface of gastric and gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) cancer cells. Zolbetuximab induces tumor cell killing through antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) and complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC). The SPOTLIGHT trial (zolbetuximab + mFOLFOX6) and GLOW trial (zolbetuximab + CAPOX) both demonstrated significant improvement in PFS and OS in CLDN18.2-positive, HER2-negative gastric/GEJ adenocarcinoma. SPOTLIGHT showed median PFS of 10.6 vs 8.7 months (HR 0.75) and OS of 18.2 vs 15.5 months (HR 0.75). CLDN18.2 positivity is assessed by the VENTANA CLDN18 (43-14A) RxDx companion diagnostic, requiring moderate-to-strong membrane staining in >=75% of tumor cells. Key adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, and decreased appetite, primarily infusion-related. Zolbetuximab represents the first CLDN18.2-targeted therapy to reach approval.
approved_indications: CLDN18.2-positive, HER2-negative locally advanced unresectable or metastatic gastric/GEJ adenocarcinoma (with chemotherapy)
mechanism_of_action: Anti-CLDN18.2 monoclonal antibody inducing ADCC and CDC

text_summary: Tarlatamab is a bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) antibody construct that simultaneously binds DLL3 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, redirecting cytotoxic T lymphocytes to kill DLL3-expressing tumor cells. Tarlatamab received FDA accelerated approval for previously treated extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC) based on the DeLLphi-301 trial, which demonstrated ORR of 40% and median DOR of 9.7 months at the recommended 10mg dose. Given the historically poor prognosis of relapsed SCLC (median OS of approximately 5-7 months with topotecan), tarlatamab represents a meaningful advance. DLL3 is expressed in approximately 80-85% of SCLC tumors but is largely absent from normal tissues. Key adverse effects include cytokine release syndrome (CRS, 53%), immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS), decreased appetite, and fatigue. Step-up dosing and hospitalization for initial doses mitigate CRS risk.
approved_indications: previously treated extensive-stage small cell lung cancer
mechanism_of_action: DLL3xCD3 bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) redirecting T cells to DLL3-positive tumor cells

text_summary: Tazemetostat is a selective, oral EZH2 (Enhancer of Zeste Homolog 2) inhibitor that blocks trimethylation of histone H3 at lysine 27 (H3K27me3), reversing epigenetic silencing of tumor suppressor genes. FDA-approved indications include relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma (FL) with EZH2 mutation (ORR 69%, CR 12%) or for patients without satisfactory alternative treatment options (ORR 34%), and metastatic or locally advanced epithelioid sarcoma not eligible for curative surgery (ORR 15%, with durable responses). In EZH2-mutant FL, the drug targets the neomorphic gain-of-function mutations (Y646N/F/H, A687V, A692V) that increase H3K27me3. In epithelioid sarcoma, SMARCB1 (INI1) loss creates a synthetic lethal dependency on EZH2. Tazemetostat is generally well tolerated with adverse effects including fatigue, nausea, upper respiratory tract infection, and musculoskeletal pain. Secondary malignancies (T-cell lymphoma, MDS/AML) have been reported rarely.
approved_indications: relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma (EZH2-mutant and wild-type), metastatic or locally advanced epithelioid sarcoma
mechanism_of_action: Selective EZH2 histone methyltransferase inhibitor (blocks H3K27 trimethylation)

text_summary: Ipilimumab is a fully human IgG1 monoclonal antibody targeting CTLA-4 (cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4), the first immune checkpoint inhibitor to demonstrate overall survival benefit in metastatic melanoma. Ipilimumab enhances T-cell priming and activation by blocking the CTLA-4 co-inhibitory receptor, which normally competes with CD28 for B7 ligand binding on antigen-presenting cells. The MDX010-20 trial demonstrated OS benefit in metastatic melanoma (10.1 vs 6.4 months). Ipilimumab's greatest clinical impact is in combination with nivolumab (anti-PD-1), with the CheckMate-067 trial showing 5-year OS of 52% in advanced melanoma. This combination is also FDA-approved in renal cell carcinoma (CheckMate-214), NSCLC (CheckMate-227/9LA), hepatocellular carcinoma, and mesothelioma. Key adverse effects are immune-related and include colitis (most common grade 3-4 toxicity), hepatitis, dermatitis, and endocrinopathies. The combination with nivolumab has higher toxicity rates (~60% grade 3-4) but achieves durable responses.
approved_indications: melanoma (adjuvant and metastatic), renal cell carcinoma (with nivolumab), NSCLC (with nivolumab), hepatocellular carcinoma (with nivolumab), mesothelioma (with nivolumab), MSI-H/dMMR CRC (with nivolumab)
mechanism_of_action: Anti-CTLA-4 monoclonal antibody (enhances T-cell priming by blocking co-inhibitory checkpoint)

text_summary: Capivasertib is a selective, oral inhibitor of all three AKT isoforms (AKT1, AKT2, AKT3) approved in combination with fulvestrant for HR-positive/HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer with one or more PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN alterations after progression on at least one endocrine-based regimen. The CAPItello-291 trial demonstrated PFS of 7.2 vs 3.6 months (HR 0.60) in the altered-pathway population and 5.5 vs 3.6 months in the overall population. Capivasertib directly inhibits AKT kinase activity, blocking downstream signaling through mTOR, FOXO, and GSK3-beta regardless of the specific upstream alteration (PIK3CA mutation, AKT1 E17K, or PTEN loss). Key adverse effects include diarrhea, rash (maculopapular), hyperglycemia, and infection. Capivasertib uses an intermittent dosing schedule (4 days on / 3 days off) to manage toxicity. The FoundationOne CDx is the companion diagnostic for identifying eligible patients.
approved_indications: HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer with PIK3CA/AKT1/PTEN alterations (with fulvestrant)
mechanism_of_action: Selective pan-AKT kinase inhibitor (blocks AKT1/2/3 catalytic activity)

text_summary: Elacestrant is an oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) approved for ER-positive/HER2-negative advanced or metastatic breast cancer with ESR1 mutations after at least one line of endocrine therapy. The EMERALD trial demonstrated PFS of 3.8 vs 1.9 months (HR 0.55) in the ESR1-mutant population compared to standard-of-care endocrine monotherapy. Elacestrant binds the estrogen receptor, induces conformational changes that promote receptor degradation via the proteasome, and blocks estrogen-dependent transcription. Unlike fulvestrant (intramuscular injection), elacestrant is orally bioavailable with once-daily dosing. Elacestrant was specifically designed to retain activity against ESR1 ligand-binding domain mutations (D538G, Y537S) that confer resistance to aromatase inhibitors. Key adverse effects include nausea, fatigue, vomiting, decreased appetite, arthralgia, and hot flashes. ESR1 mutation testing by ctDNA liquid biopsy is recommended to identify eligible patients. Elacestrant is the first oral SERD to receive FDA approval.
approved_indications: ESR1-mutant, ER+/HER2- advanced breast cancer (after prior endocrine therapy)
mechanism_of_action: Oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) with activity against ESR1 mutations

text_summary: Repotrectinib is a next-generation, macrocyclic tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting ROS1 and NTRK with potent activity against solvent-front resistance mutations that confer resistance to first-generation ROS1 inhibitors. FDA approval for ROS1-positive NSCLC was based on the TRIDENT-1 trial, which demonstrated ORR of 79% in TKI-naive patients and 38% in patients previously treated with one prior ROS1 TKI. Notably, repotrectinib showed an ORR of 59% against the ROS1 G2032R solvent-front mutation, the most common mechanism of acquired resistance to crizotinib and entrectinib. The compact macrocyclic structure of repotrectinib enables binding in the constrained ATP pocket created by solvent-front mutations. Repotrectinib also has activity against NTRK fusions including those with solvent-front resistance mutations. Key adverse effects include dizziness, dysgeusia, peripheral neuropathy, constipation, and ataxia. CNS adverse effects are generally mild and transient.
approved_indications: ROS1-positive NSCLC (TKI-naive and previously treated)
mechanism_of_action: Next-generation macrocyclic ROS1/TRK inhibitor with activity against solvent-front resistance mutations

text_summary: Ruxolitinib is an oral selective JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor and the first FDA-approved therapy for myelofibrosis (MF). Approval was based on the COMFORT-I and COMFORT-II trials, which demonstrated significant reductions in splenomegaly (>=35% spleen volume reduction in 41.9% vs 0.7% for placebo) and improvements in constitutional symptoms in intermediate-2 and high-risk MF patients. Ruxolitinib is also approved for polycythemia vera (PV) inadequately controlled by or intolerant to hydroxyurea (RESPONSE trial: 21% complete hematologic response vs 1% for best available therapy) and for steroid-refractory acute and chronic graft-versus-host disease (REACH trials). Ruxolitinib inhibits JAK2 V617F (present in ~60% of MF and ~95% of PV) and JAK2 exon 12 mutations by blocking the ATP-binding site of JAK1/2 kinases, suppressing downstream STAT3/5 signaling and reducing inflammatory cytokine production. Key adverse effects include thrombocytopenia, anemia, neutropenia, and increased infection risk. Abrupt discontinuation can cause a cytokine rebound flare.
approved_indications: myelofibrosis, polycythemia vera (hydroxyurea-resistant), steroid-refractory acute and chronic GVHD
mechanism_of_action: Selective JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor (blocks JAK-STAT signaling and inflammatory cytokine production)

text_summary: Gilteritinib is an oral selective FLT3 inhibitor with activity against both FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD (D835) mutations, approved for relapsed/refractory FLT3-mutant AML. The ADMIRAL trial demonstrated superior OS for gilteritinib versus salvage chemotherapy (9.3 vs 5.6 months) with complete remission (CR + CRh) of 34% versus 15.3%. Unlike midostaurin, gilteritinib is a selective FLT3 inhibitor with minimal off-target kinase activity, resulting in a more favorable safety profile. Gilteritinib also inhibits AXL, which may contribute to overcoming FLT3 inhibitor resistance mediated by AXL upregulation. Key adverse effects include hepatotoxicity (elevated transaminases), myalgia/arthralgia, fatigue, and differentiation syndrome (in ~3% of patients). FLT3 mutation testing by PCR or NGS is required at diagnosis and relapse. Resistance mechanisms include secondary FLT3 on-target mutations and RAS/MAPK pathway activation.
approved_indications: FLT3-mutant relapsed/refractory AML
mechanism_of_action: Selective FLT3/AXL inhibitor (active against FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD D835 mutations)

text_summary: Midostaurin is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting FLT3, KIT, PDGFR, VEGFR, and PKC, approved in combination with standard induction and consolidation chemotherapy for newly diagnosed FLT3-mutant AML and for advanced systemic mastocytosis (advSM). The landmark RATIFY trial demonstrated that adding midostaurin to 7+3 induction chemotherapy improved 4-year OS from 44% to 51% (HR 0.78, p=0.009) in FLT3-mutant AML, establishing it as the first targeted agent proven to improve survival in AML when combined with intensive chemotherapy. Midostaurin is active against both FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD mutations. For advSM, midostaurin showed ORR of 60% in the D2201 trial. Key adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, mucositis, febrile neutropenia, and QTc prolongation. Midostaurin is a less selective FLT3 inhibitor than gilteritinib, and its primary role is in the frontline FLT3-mutant AML setting combined with chemotherapy.
targets: FLT3, KIT, PDGFRA, PDGFRB, VEGFR2, PKC
approved_indications: newly diagnosed FLT3-mutant AML (with chemotherapy), advanced systemic mastocytosis
mechanism_of_action: Multi-kinase inhibitor (FLT3/KIT/PDGFR/PKC) with broad activity against FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD

text_summary: Venetoclax is a selective, oral BCL-2 inhibitor that restores apoptosis in cancer cells dependent on BCL-2 for survival. In CLL, venetoclax plus obinutuzumab (CLL14 trial) or rituximab (MURANO trial) has become a standard first-line and relapsed therapy respectively, with fixed-duration treatment enabling treatment-free remissions. In AML, venetoclax combined with azacitidine (VIALE-A trial: median OS 14.7 vs 9.6 months) or low-dose cytarabine (VIALE-C) transformed the treatment of older/unfit AML patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy. Venetoclax is also approved for relapsed/refractory CLL as monotherapy (after prior ibrutinib/idelalisib). The drug mimics the BH3-only protein to displace pro-apoptotic BAX/BAK from BCL-2, triggering mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization and caspase-dependent apoptosis. Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) is the most concerning adverse effect, requiring gradual dose ramp-up over 5 weeks with prophylactic hydration and anti-hyperuricemics. Neutropenia is common.
approved_indications: CLL/SLL (with obinutuzumab or rituximab), newly diagnosed AML (with azacitidine or low-dose cytarabine in unfit patients)
mechanism_of_action: Selective BCL-2 inhibitor (BH3 mimetic restoring mitochondrial apoptosis)

text_summary: Imatinib is a first-generation oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor that revolutionized the treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) and gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST). In CML, the IRIS trial demonstrated 5-year OS of 89% for imatinib versus interferon-alpha/cytarabine, transforming CML from a fatal disease to a manageable chronic condition. Imatinib targets the BCR-ABL fusion oncoprotein by binding the inactive conformation (type II inhibitor), blocking ATP binding and downstream signaling. In GIST, imatinib targets KIT and PDGFRA mutations, with the B2222 trial establishing imatinib as the standard of care for unresectable/metastatic KIT-positive GIST (ORR ~50%, median PFS ~24 months). Also approved for Ph+ ALL, MDS/MPD with PDGFR rearrangements, and dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans. Key adverse effects include edema, nausea, muscle cramps, rash, and myelosuppression. CML patients achieving deep molecular response (MR4.5) may attempt treatment-free remission. Generic imatinib is now widely available.
approved_indications: CML (all phases), Ph+ ALL, KIT-positive GIST, MDS/MPD with PDGFR rearrangements, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans
mechanism_of_action: Type II tyrosine kinase inhibitor (binds inactive conformation of BCR-ABL/KIT/PDGFR)

text_summary: Dasatinib is a second-generation, multi-kinase inhibitor targeting BCR-ABL, SRC family kinases, KIT, EPHB2, and PDGFR. Dasatinib is approved for CML in chronic phase as first-line therapy (DASISION trial: 5-year MMR 76% vs 64% for imatinib) and for CML resistant or intolerant to prior therapy including imatinib. Dasatinib also has accelerated approval for Ph+ ALL. Unlike imatinib, dasatinib binds both the active and inactive conformations of ABL kinase, providing activity against most BCR-ABL kinase domain mutations that confer imatinib resistance, with the notable exception of the T315I gatekeeper mutation. Dasatinib is approximately 325-fold more potent than imatinib against unmutated BCR-ABL in vitro. Key adverse effects include pleural effusions (a distinctive toxicity of dasatinib, occurring in ~28% of patients), pulmonary arterial hypertension, myelosuppression, and QTc prolongation. SRC inhibition may contribute to both antitumor activity and unique toxicity profile.
approved_indications: CML (first-line and resistant/intolerant to imatinib), Ph+ ALL
mechanism_of_action: Multi-kinase inhibitor binding active and inactive BCR-ABL conformations (also inhibits SRC family kinases)

text_summary: Ponatinib is a third-generation oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor specifically designed to overcome the BCR-ABL T315I gatekeeper mutation, which is resistant to all first- and second-generation TKIs (imatinib, dasatinib, nilotinib, bosutinib). The OPTIC trial established a response-based dosing strategy (45mg reduced to 15mg after BCR-ABL <=1%), achieving 2-year PFS of 78% in T315I-mutant CML patients. Ponatinib's triple-bond linker enables it to avoid the steric clash with the bulky isoleucine gatekeeper residue. Also active against all other clinically relevant BCR-ABL mutations and approved for resistant/intolerant CML and Ph+ ALL. Key adverse effects are primarily vascular: arterial occlusive events (myocardial infarction, stroke, peripheral vascular disease) occurring in ~25% of patients at the original 45mg dose, leading to dose-optimization strategies. Other adverse effects include pancreatitis, hepatotoxicity, and hypertension. Ponatinib carries a boxed warning for vascular occlusion and heart failure.
targets: BCR-ABL (including T315I), VEGFR, PDGFR, FGFR, SRC
approved_indications: CML with T315I mutation or resistant/intolerant to >=2 prior TKIs, Ph+ ALL with T315I or resistant/intolerant
mechanism_of_action: Third-generation BCR-ABL inhibitor with activity against T315I gatekeeper mutation (pan-ABL kinase inhibitor)

text_summary: Ibrutinib is a first-in-class oral irreversible Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor that transformed the treatment of B-cell malignancies. Approved for CLL/SLL (first-line and relapsed), mantle cell lymphoma (MCL), marginal zone lymphoma (MZL), Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (WM), and chronic GVHD. In CLL, the RESONATE-2 trial showed superior PFS for ibrutinib versus chlorambucil in treatment-naive older patients, with 5-year PFS of 70%. The ECOG-1912 trial demonstrated ibrutinib-rituximab superiority over FCR in younger CLL patients. Ibrutinib covalently binds C481 in the BTK active site, irreversibly blocking BCR signaling, adhesion, migration, and proliferation. Key adverse effects include atrial fibrillation (~10-15%), hypertension, bleeding (inhibits collagen-mediated platelet aggregation via TEC kinase), arthralgia, and infection risk. Resistance develops primarily through BTK C481S mutation. Second-generation BTK inhibitors (acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) were designed to reduce off-target toxicity.
approved_indications: CLL/SLL, mantle cell lymphoma, marginal zone lymphoma, Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, chronic GVHD
mechanism_of_action: Irreversible covalent BTK inhibitor (blocks B-cell receptor signaling at C481)

text_summary: Acalabrutinib is a second-generation, highly selective irreversible BTK inhibitor designed to improve on ibrutinib's safety profile by minimizing off-target kinase inhibition (particularly TEC, EGFR, and ITK). Approved for CLL/SLL (first-line and relapsed) and previously treated MCL. The ELEVATE-TN trial demonstrated superior PFS for acalabrutinib with or without obinutuzumab versus chlorambucil-obinutuzumab in treatment-naive CLL (estimated 4-year PFS 87%). The ELEVATE-RR head-to-head trial versus ibrutinib in relapsed CLL showed non-inferior PFS with significantly fewer atrial fibrillation events (9.4% vs 16.0%), hypertension, and bleeding. Acalabrutinib covalently modifies BTK C481, similar to ibrutinib, but with greater BTK selectivity. Key adverse effects include headache (common early, typically self-resolving), diarrhea, neutropenia, and upper respiratory tract infection. Atrial fibrillation risk is lower than ibrutinib but still present. Same BTK C481S resistance mutation applies.
approved_indications: CLL/SLL (first-line and relapsed), previously treated mantle cell lymphoma
mechanism_of_action: Second-generation selective irreversible BTK inhibitor (improved selectivity over ibrutinib)

text_summary: Zanubrutinib is a second-generation, highly selective irreversible BTK inhibitor optimized for complete and sustained BTK occupancy with minimized off-target kinase activity. Approved for Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (WM), relapsed/refractory MCL, MZL, and CLL/SLL. The ALPINE trial (zanubrutinib vs ibrutinib in relapsed CLL) demonstrated superior PFS (HR 0.65) and ORR (83.5% vs 74.2%) with significantly fewer cardiac adverse events (atrial fibrillation 2.5% vs 10.1%, hypertension 14.8% vs 11.1%). In WM, the ASPEN trial showed higher rates of VGPR/CR (28% vs 19%) versus ibrutinib. Zanubrutinib was specifically designed with lower EGFR, TEC, and ITK inhibition, contributing to reduced rash, diarrhea, and platelet dysfunction. Key adverse effects include neutropenia (more common than ibrutinib), upper respiratory tract infection, bruising, and diarrhea. The MYD88 L265P and CXCR4 mutation status in WM guides expected response depth. Zanubrutinib is increasingly favored over ibrutinib due to superior efficacy-safety balance.
approved_indications: CLL/SLL, Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, mantle cell lymphoma, marginal zone lymphoma
mechanism_of_action: Second-generation selective irreversible BTK inhibitor (optimized BTK occupancy and minimal off-target effects)

text_summary: Enzalutamide is a second-generation oral androgen receptor (AR) inhibitor approved across the spectrum of prostate cancer from metastatic castration-resistant (mCRPC) to metastatic castration-sensitive (mCSPC) and non-metastatic CRPC (nmCRPC). The PREVAIL trial in chemotherapy-naive mCRPC showed OS of 35.3 vs 31.3 months (HR 0.77), and the ARCHES/ENZAMET trials established enzalutamide plus ADT in mCSPC with significant OS improvement. The PROSPER trial demonstrated metastasis-free survival benefit in nmCRPC (36.6 vs 14.7 months). Enzalutamide competitively inhibits androgen binding to the AR, blocks AR nuclear translocation, and inhibits AR-DNA binding and co-activator recruitment. Unlike first-generation antiandrogens (bicalutamide), enzalutamide has no agonist activity. Key adverse effects include fatigue, hypertension, seizure risk (rare, ~0.5%), cognitive impairment, and hot flashes. AR mutations (F876L, T878A) and AR-V7 splice variant are resistance mechanisms.
approved_indications: metastatic CRPC, non-metastatic CRPC, metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer
mechanism_of_action: Second-generation AR antagonist (blocks ligand binding, nuclear translocation, and DNA binding)

text_summary: Abiraterone acetate is an oral, irreversible inhibitor of CYP17A1 (17-alpha-hydroxylase/C17,20-lyase), a critical enzyme in androgen biosynthesis, approved for metastatic prostate cancer. The COU-AA-301 trial in post-docetaxel mCRPC demonstrated OS of 15.8 vs 11.2 months (HR 0.74), and COU-AA-302 in chemotherapy-naive mCRPC showed OS of 34.7 vs 30.3 months. The LATITUDE and STAMPEDE trials established abiraterone in combination with ADT for high-risk mCSPC, with LATITUDE showing OS of 53.3 vs 36.5 months. Abiraterone blocks androgen synthesis in the adrenals, testes, and intratumorally, reducing testosterone to below conventional castration levels. Must be co-administered with prednisone/prednisolone to counteract mineralocorticoid excess from CYP17A1 inhibition (upstream cortisol block increases ACTH, driving mineralocorticoid precursors). Key adverse effects include hypertension, hypokalemia, fluid retention, hepatotoxicity, and cardiac events. Generic formulations are now available.
approved_indications: metastatic CRPC (pre- and post-docetaxel), metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (high-risk)
mechanism_of_action: Irreversible CYP17A1 inhibitor (blocks androgen biosynthesis in adrenal, testicular, and intratumoral compartments)

text_summary: Fulvestrant is an intramuscular selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) that binds, blocks, and promotes degradation of the estrogen receptor (ER). Approved for HR-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer as monotherapy in postmenopausal women progressing on antiestrogen therapy and as the backbone partner for multiple targeted combinations (CDK4/6 inhibitors, PI3K inhibitors, AKT inhibitors). The FALCON trial demonstrated superior PFS for fulvestrant versus anastrozole as first-line therapy in ER-positive advanced breast cancer (16.6 vs 13.8 months). Fulvestrant is a 7-alpha-alkylamide derivative of estradiol that induces ER conformational changes promoting proteasomal degradation, completely abolishing ER-mediated transcription unlike tamoxifen (partial agonist). Administered as 500mg intramuscular injection monthly (with loading dose). Key adverse effects include injection site reactions, nausea, bone pain, and arthralgia. Fulvestrant is the primary combination partner for palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib, alpelisib, and capivasertib in the metastatic setting.
approved_indications: HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer (monotherapy and combination backbone)
mechanism_of_action: Selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD) promoting proteasomal ER degradation

text_summary: Ripretinib is a broad-spectrum KIT and PDGFRA switch-control kinase inhibitor approved for advanced GIST after prior treatment with three or more kinase inhibitors including imatinib. The INVICTUS trial demonstrated PFS of 6.3 vs 1.0 months (HR 0.15) and OS of 15.1 vs 6.6 months versus placebo in fourth-line GIST. The INTRIGUE trial compared ripretinib to sunitinib in second-line GIST, showing similar PFS overall but potential benefit in KIT exon 11-mutant patients. Ripretinib has a dual mechanism: it inhibits KIT kinase activity through switch-control inhibition of the activation loop (type II-like binding) and also employs a switch-pocket binding moiety that forces the kinase into an inactive conformation. This dual mechanism provides broad activity against primary KIT exon 9, 11, 13, and 17 mutations and secondary resistance mutations in the ATP-binding pocket and activation loop. Key adverse effects include alopecia, fatigue, nausea, myalgia, hand-foot syndrome, and hypophosphatemia.
approved_indications: advanced GIST (fourth-line after >=3 prior kinase inhibitors)
mechanism_of_action: Broad-spectrum KIT/PDGFRA switch-control kinase inhibitor (dual mechanism: kinase + switch-pocket inhibition)

text_summary: Tucatinib is an oral, highly selective HER2 tyrosine kinase inhibitor approved in combination with trastuzumab and capecitabine for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer after prior trastuzumab, pertuzumab, and T-DM1. The HER2CLIMB trial demonstrated PFS of 7.8 vs 5.6 months (HR 0.54) and OS of 21.9 vs 17.4 months (HR 0.66). Uniquely, tucatinib showed significant activity in patients with brain metastases, with intracranial ORR of 47% and CNS-PFS of 9.9 vs 4.2 months, addressing a major unmet need. Tucatinib's high selectivity for HER2 over EGFR (>1000-fold) minimizes EGFR-related toxicities (diarrhea, rash) compared to less selective HER2 TKIs like lapatinib and neratinib. Also approved in combination with trastuzumab for RAS wild-type, HER2-positive metastatic colorectal cancer (MOUNTAINEER trial: ORR 38.1%). Key adverse effects include diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome, nausea, hepatotoxicity, and fatigue.
approved_indications: HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer (with trastuzumab/capecitabine), HER2-positive metastatic CRC (with trastuzumab)
mechanism_of_action: Highly selective HER2 tyrosine kinase inhibitor (>1000-fold selectivity over EGFR)

text_summary: Pertuzumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting HER2 subdomain II (the dimerization domain), preventing HER2 heterodimerization with other HER family members (HER1, HER3, HER4). This mechanism is complementary to trastuzumab, which binds HER2 subdomain IV. The CLEOPATRA trial established pertuzumab plus trastuzumab plus docetaxel as first-line therapy for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer with median OS of 56.5 months versus 40.8 months for trastuzumab plus docetaxel alone. The APHINITY trial demonstrated DFS benefit for the addition of pertuzumab to adjuvant trastuzumab-chemotherapy in high-risk HER2-positive early breast cancer. In the neoadjuvant setting (NeoSphere trial), pertuzumab-trastuzumab-docetaxel achieved pCR of 45.8% versus 29% for trastuzumab-docetaxel. Pertuzumab induces ADCC and blocks ligand-activated HER2-HER3 signaling, a key driver of PI3K/AKT pathway activation. Key adverse effects include diarrhea, rash, and cardiac dysfunction (requires LVEF monitoring). The fixed-dose combination of pertuzumab/trastuzumab/hyaluronidase (Phesgo) enables subcutaneous administration.
approved_indications: HER2-positive breast cancer (metastatic first-line, neoadjuvant, adjuvant) in combination with trastuzumab
mechanism_of_action: Anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody targeting dimerization domain (blocks HER2-HER3 heterodimerization and induces ADCC)

text_summary: Belzutifan is a first-in-class oral HIF-2-alpha inhibitor approved for von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease-associated renal cell carcinoma (RCC), CNS hemangioblastomas, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors not requiring immediate surgery. Study 004 demonstrated ORR of 49% in VHL-associated RCC, with 89% of evaluable CNS hemangioblastomas and 77% of pancreatic lesions showing reduction. Also approved in combination with pembro for advanced RCC after prior therapies (LITESPARK-005 trial). Belzutifan directly binds HIF-2-alpha, blocking its dimerization with ARNT (HIF-1-beta) and preventing transcription of HIF-2-alpha target genes including VEGF, PDGF, cyclin D1, and EPO. VHL loss-of-function (seen in ~90% of clear cell RCC) stabilizes HIF-2-alpha, making this a genotype-directed therapy. Key adverse effects include anemia (expected on-target effect from reduced EPO transcription, ~90% any grade), hypoxia, fatigue, and dizziness. Erythrocyte monitoring is essential.
approved_indications: VHL disease-associated RCC, CNS hemangioblastomas, pancreatic NETs; advanced RCC after prior therapy (with pembrolizumab)
mechanism_of_action: HIF-2-alpha inhibitor (blocks HIF-2-alpha/ARNT dimerization and HIF-2-alpha-dependent transcription)

text_summary: Cabozantinib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR2, MET, AXL, RET, KIT, and FLT3, approved for multiple cancer types. In advanced RCC, the METEOR trial (second-line) demonstrated PFS of 7.4 vs 3.9 months and OS of 21.4 vs 16.5 months versus everolimus, and the CABOSUN trial (first-line) showed PFS improvement versus sunitinib. Cabozantinib in combination with nivolumab (CheckMate-9ER) is now a first-line option for advanced RCC with PFS of 16.6 months. In hepatocellular carcinoma, the CELESTIAL trial showed OS of 10.2 vs 8.0 months versus placebo in the second-line setting. Also approved for progressive, metastatic medullary thyroid cancer (EXAM trial: PFS 11.2 vs 4.0 months) and DTC. Cabozantinib's MET/AXL inhibition differentiates it from other VEGFR TKIs by overcoming resistance driven by MET and AXL upregulation in the tumor microenvironment. Key adverse effects include diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome, hypertension, fatigue, and hepatotoxicity.
targets: VEGFR2, MET, AXL, RET, KIT, FLT3
approved_indications: advanced RCC (mono and with nivolumab), hepatocellular carcinoma, medullary thyroid cancer, differentiated thyroid cancer
mechanism_of_action: Multi-kinase inhibitor (VEGFR/MET/AXL/RET) overcoming resistance via dual angiogenesis and MET/AXL pathway blockade

text_summary: Lenvatinib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFRA, KIT, and RET, approved across multiple tumor types. In differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC), the SELECT trial showed PFS of 18.3 vs 3.6 months versus placebo (HR 0.21). In hepatocellular carcinoma, the REFLECT trial established lenvatinib as non-inferior to sorafenib in first-line with higher ORR (24% vs 9%). Lenvatinib combined with pembrolizumab is approved for advanced endometrial carcinoma (KEYNOTE-775: OS 18.3 vs 11.4 months in non-MSI-H patients) and advanced RCC (KEYNOTE-426 and CLEAR trials). The FGFR inhibitory activity distinguishes lenvatinib from other VEGFR TKIs and may contribute to its efficacy in thyroid cancer and anti-angiogenic resistant tumors. Key adverse effects include hypertension (the most common serious toxicity), diarrhea, decreased appetite, weight loss, fatigue, and proteinuria. Dose reductions are frequently required (~60% of patients). Hepatotoxicity and arterial thromboembolic events are important safety concerns.
targets: VEGFR1, VEGFR2, VEGFR3, FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3, FGFR4, PDGFRA, KIT, RET
approved_indications: differentiated thyroid cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, advanced endometrial carcinoma (with pembrolizumab), advanced RCC (with pembrolizumab)
mechanism_of_action: Multi-kinase inhibitor (VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR/KIT/RET) with broad anti-angiogenic and anti-tumor activity

text_summary: Durvalumab is a human IgG1-kappa monoclonal antibody targeting PD-L1 with an engineered Fc domain to reduce ADCC/CDC. The PACIFIC trial established durvalumab as the standard consolidation therapy after chemoradiation in unresectable stage III NSCLC, demonstrating dramatic improvements in PFS (17.2 vs 5.6 months) and 5-year OS rate of 42.9% versus 33.4% for placebo. In extensive-stage SCLC, the CASPIAN trial showed OS improvement with durvalumab plus platinum-etoposide versus chemotherapy alone (13.0 vs 10.3 months). Durvalumab is also approved for unresectable HCC (HIMALAYA trial: durvalumab + tremelimumab single-dose), biliary tract cancer (TOPAZ-1: with gemcitabine/cisplatin, OS 12.8 vs 11.5 months), and endometrial cancer. In the perioperative setting, the AEGEAN trial showed improved pathologic response and EFS in resectable NSCLC. Key adverse effects include pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, colitis, and rash. The SP263 PD-L1 IHC assay is the companion diagnostic.
approved_indications: unresectable stage III NSCLC (consolidation), ES-SCLC, HCC (with tremelimumab), biliary tract cancer, endometrial cancer
mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-L1 monoclonal antibody (blocks PD-L1/PD-1 and PD-L1/CD80 interactions with engineered Fc)

text_summary: Dostarlimab is a humanized anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody approved for dMMR recurrent or advanced endometrial cancer (as monotherapy after progression on platinum-containing chemotherapy) and dMMR recurrent or advanced solid tumors (tissue-agnostic, after prior treatment). The GARNET trial demonstrated ORR of 44.7% in dMMR/MSI-H endometrial cancer with durable responses (median DOR not reached). In a landmark proof-of-concept study at Memorial Sloan Kettering, dostarlimab monotherapy achieved clinical complete response in 100% (12/12) of dMMR locally advanced rectal cancer patients, with no patients requiring subsequent chemoradiation or surgery during follow-up, representing a potential paradigm shift for organ preservation in rectal cancer. Key adverse effects include fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, and immune-related adverse events (thyroid dysfunction, hepatitis, colitis, pneumonitis). dMMR/MSI-H testing by IHC and/or PCR/NGS is required for patient selection.
approved_indications: dMMR endometrial cancer, dMMR solid tumors (tissue-agnostic)
mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody (restores T cell anti-tumor immunity)

text_summary: Cemiplimab is a human IgG4 monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1, with distinct approvals including the first immunotherapy for advanced cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC). The EMPOWER-CSCC-1 trial demonstrated ORR of 44% in metastatic CSCC and 44% in locally advanced CSCC with durable responses. In NSCLC, the EMPOWER-Lung-1 trial showed OS benefit for cemiplimab versus chemotherapy in first-line PD-L1 >=50% NSCLC (OS not reached vs 14.2 months, HR 0.57). Also approved for locally advanced or metastatic basal cell carcinoma (BCC) progressing on or intolerant to hedgehog pathway inhibitor therapy (EMPOWER-BCC-1: ORR 31%), filling an important gap for advanced BCC. In cervical cancer, the EMPOWER-Cervical-1 trial showed OS improvement versus investigator's choice chemotherapy. Key adverse effects include fatigue, rash, diarrhea, musculoskeletal pain, and immune-mediated adverse events. Cemiplimab's niche is primarily in CSCC and BCC where other checkpoint inhibitors lack specific approvals.
approved_indications: advanced cutaneous SCC, NSCLC (PD-L1 >=50%), advanced BCC (post-HHI), cervical cancer
mechanism_of_action: Anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody (restores anti-tumor T cell immunity)

text_summary: Trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1, ado-trastuzumab emtansine) is an antibody-drug conjugate composed of trastuzumab linked to the microtubule inhibitor DM1 (emtansine, a maytansine derivative) via a non-cleavable MCC linker with an average drug-to-antibody ratio of 3.5:1. The EMILIA trial established T-DM1 as second-line therapy for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer with PFS of 9.6 vs 6.4 months and OS of 30.9 vs 25.1 months versus lapatinib plus capecitabine. The KATHERINE trial demonstrated T-DM1 in the adjuvant setting for patients with residual invasive HER2-positive breast cancer after neoadjuvant therapy, reducing recurrence risk by 50% (3-year iDFS 88.3% vs 77.0%). Unlike trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd), T-DM1 uses a non-cleavable linker and has minimal bystander killing effect, limiting activity to cells with high HER2 expression. Key adverse effects include thrombocytopenia, hepatotoxicity (elevated transaminases), fatigue, and peripheral neuropathy. T-DM1 has been partly superseded by T-DXd in the metastatic setting but remains important in adjuvant post-neoadjuvant residual disease.
approved_indications: HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer (after prior trastuzumab/taxane), adjuvant HER2-positive breast cancer with residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy
mechanism_of_action: Anti-HER2 antibody-drug conjugate with DM1 microtubule inhibitor payload (non-cleavable linker)

text_summary: Tisotumab vedotin is an antibody-drug conjugate targeting tissue factor (TF/CD142/F3), a transmembrane protein overexpressed in multiple solid tumors that also plays a role in tumor-associated coagulation and angiogenesis. The ADC links an anti-tissue factor antibody to MMAE via a protease-cleavable valine-citrulline linker. Tisotumab vedotin received accelerated FDA approval for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer with disease progression on or after chemotherapy, based on the innovaTV 204 trial demonstrating ORR of 24% with median DOR of 8.3 months. The confirmatory innovaTV 301 trial showed superior OS versus investigator's choice chemotherapy (11.5 vs 9.5 months, HR 0.70), leading to regular approval. Key adverse effects include ocular toxicity (conjunctivitis, corneal adverse events requiring eye care protocol with lubricant eye drops, vasoconstrictive eye drops, and cold packs during infusion), epistaxis, peripheral neuropathy, and fatigue. Tissue factor is expressed in >50% of many solid tumors, supporting investigation across other indications.
approved_indications: recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer (after prior chemotherapy)
mechanism_of_action: Anti-tissue factor antibody-drug conjugate with MMAE microtubule inhibitor payload

text_summary: Niraparib is an oral PARP1/2 inhibitor approved for maintenance treatment of recurrent epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer after complete or partial response to platinum-based chemotherapy, and as first-line maintenance in advanced ovarian cancer. The NOVA trial demonstrated PFS benefit in both BRCA-mutant (21.0 vs 5.5 months) and BRCA wild-type HRD-positive (12.9 vs 3.8 months) cohorts. The PRIMA/ENGOT-OV26 trial established first-line niraparib maintenance regardless of BRCA/HRD status (overall population PFS 13.8 vs 8.2 months), with greatest benefit in HRD-positive patients (21.9 vs 10.4 months). Niraparib also received approval for late-line treatment of BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer based on QUADRA. Unlike olaparib, niraparib does not require BRCA mutation for the maintenance indication. Key adverse effects include thrombocytopenia (a distinguishing toxicity, 33.8% grade 3/4, requiring individualized starting dose based on baseline platelet count and body weight), anemia, neutropenia, fatigue, and hypertension. MDS/AML risk is approximately 1-2%.
approved_indications: ovarian cancer maintenance (first-line and recurrent, regardless of BRCA status), BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer treatment
mechanism_of_action: PARP1/2 inhibitor with PARP trapping activity (synthetic lethality with HRD)

text_summary: Talazoparib is an oral PARP1/2 inhibitor with the most potent PARP trapping activity among approved PARP inhibitors, approximately 100-fold greater than olaparib. The EMBRACA trial demonstrated PFS of 8.6 vs 5.6 months (HR 0.54) for talazoparib versus physician's choice chemotherapy in germline BRCA-mutant HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer, with improved patient-reported outcomes (significant delay in time to deterioration of global health status). Talazoparib's enhanced PARP trapping (stabilization of PARP-DNA complexes) is thought to be the primary mechanism of cytotoxicity rather than catalytic PARP inhibition alone. This potent trapping also contributes to its hematologic toxicity profile. Key adverse effects include anemia (39% grade 3/4, the most common dose-limiting toxicity), neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, fatigue, and headache. The ELATIO trial is evaluating talazoparib combined with enzalutamide in HRR-deficient mCRPC. Talazoparib requires dose adjustment for moderate renal impairment and strong P-gp inhibitors.
approved_indications: germline BRCA-mutant HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer
mechanism_of_action: PARP1/2 inhibitor with highest PARP trapping potency (stabilizes cytotoxic PARP-DNA complexes)

text_summary: Palbociclib is the first FDA-approved oral CDK4/6 inhibitor, transforming the treatment of HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer. The PALOMA-2 trial demonstrated PFS of 24.8 vs 14.5 months for palbociclib plus letrozole versus letrozole alone in first-line metastatic disease, and PALOMA-3 showed PFS of 9.5 vs 4.6 months with palbociclib plus fulvestrant in the second-line setting. Although neither trial demonstrated overall survival benefit (unusual for the class, as ribociclib showed OS improvement), palbociclib remains widely used based on PFS benefit and tolerability. CDK4/6 inhibition blocks retinoblastoma (Rb) protein phosphorylation, preventing E2F-mediated transcription and causing G1 cell cycle arrest. Rb loss or mutation is a resistance mechanism. Key adverse effects include neutropenia (grade 3/4 in ~66%, primarily hematologic toxicity managed with dose holds), leukopenia, fatigue, and nausea. Unlike ribociclib, palbociclib does not cause QTc prolongation, and unlike abemaciclib, it rarely causes diarrhea. Administered 3 weeks on / 1 week off.
approved_indications: HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer (with aromatase inhibitor or fulvestrant)
mechanism_of_action: Selective CDK4/6 inhibitor (blocks Rb phosphorylation and G1-S cell cycle transition)

text_summary: Ribociclib is an oral CDK4/6 inhibitor and the only agent in its class to demonstrate overall survival benefit in multiple phase III trials in HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer. The MONALEESA-7 trial in premenopausal/perimenopausal women showed OS of 58.7 vs 48.0 months (HR 0.76), the first CDK4/6 inhibitor to improve OS in this population. MONALEESA-3 (with fulvestrant) showed OS of 53.7 vs 41.5 months in first/second-line postmenopausal patients, and MONALEESA-2 (with letrozole) showed PFS of 25.3 vs 16.0 months. Ribociclib is now also approved in the adjuvant setting for high-risk early HR+/HER2- breast cancer based on the NATALEE trial (iDFS HR 0.749), making it the first CDK4/6 inhibitor approved in early breast cancer. Like palbociclib, ribociclib inhibits CDK4/6 to prevent Rb phosphorylation and cell cycle progression. Key adverse effects include neutropenia, hepatotoxicity (elevated transaminases), QTc prolongation (unique to ribociclib, requires ECG monitoring), and nausea. Administered 3 weeks on / 1 week off.
approved_indications: HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer (with endocrine therapy), high-risk early HR+/HER2- breast cancer (adjuvant)
mechanism_of_action: Selective CDK4/6 inhibitor (blocks G1-S transition) with demonstrated overall survival benefit

text_summary: Abemaciclib is an oral CDK4/6 inhibitor with unique pharmacologic properties that distinguish it from palbociclib and ribociclib: greater CDK4 selectivity (over CDK6), continuous dosing (no off-week), ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and single-agent activity in breast cancer. The MONARCH-3 trial demonstrated PFS of 28.2 vs 14.8 months with abemaciclib plus AI in first-line HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer. The monarchE trial established abemaciclib in the adjuvant setting for high-risk HR+/HER2- early breast cancer with node-positive disease (4-year iDFS 85.8% vs 79.4%), with the first CDK4/6 inhibitor to show adjuvant benefit. Abemaciclib monotherapy (MONARCH-1) showed ORR of 19.7% in heavily pretreated patients, the only CDK4/6 inhibitor active as monotherapy. Abemaciclib also shows activity against brain metastases. Key adverse effects include diarrhea (the most common toxicity, ~90% any grade, distinct from other CDK4/6 inhibitors), fatigue, neutropenia (less severe than palbociclib/ribociclib), nausea, and venous thromboembolism. Administered continuously twice daily.
approved_indications: HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer (with endocrine therapy or monotherapy), high-risk HR+/HER2- early breast cancer (adjuvant)
mechanism_of_action: CDK4/6 inhibitor with greater CDK4 selectivity, CNS penetration, and single-agent activity (continuous dosing)
